record_office_terminology.land_records: Register of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens, plats -- one per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions, mental health commitments, name changes)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from county organization; state registration from 1867)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1867 (statewide vital records registration; compliance improved after 1900; modern records from Michigan Department of Health and Human Services)

burned_county_authority: Archives of Michigan, Lansing -- Michigan has 5 burned counties with significant record losses: Clare (1877 courthouse fire), Huron (1881 Great Thumb Fire), Ontonagon (1896 fire), Sanilac (1881 Great Thumb Fire), and Tuscola (1881 Great Thumb Fire, lesser losses). The Archives of Michigan holds microfilmed records, tax rolls, and supplementary documentation that may fill gaps. BLM GLO land patents are unaffected by county-level fires.
administrative_notes: Michigan has 83 counties across two peninsulas -- the Lower Peninsula (68 counties) and the Upper Peninsula (15 counties), connected by the Mackinac Bridge (1957). Michigan Territory was established in 1805 from Indiana Territory, with Detroit as the capital. Statehood was achieved January 26, 1837 -- the Toledo War boundary dispute with Ohio resulted in Michigan receiving the Upper Peninsula as compensation for the Toledo Strip. Michigan is a public land state -- original land was disposed through federal land offices, and patents are available through BLM GLO. The state has strong French colonial heritage (Detroit founded 1701, Mackinac fur trade), Native American treaty history (Ottawa, Ojibwe/Chippewa, Potawatomi), and diverse immigrant communities (Finnish, Dutch, Polish, German, Cornish, Italian in mining and industrial areas). The automobile industry transformed southeastern Michigan in the early 1900s and attracted the Great Migration of African Americans from the South. The Archives of Michigan (formerly State Archives of Michigan) in Lansing and the Library of Michigan are the primary state-level repositories. The Burton Historical Collection at the Detroit Public Library is one of America's premier genealogical research libraries.

core_repositories.description: Michigan's state archives, part of the Michigan History Center. Holds state government records, county records on microfilm, vital records (births, deaths, marriages from 1867), military records, naturalization records, prison records, land records, tax rolls, and territorial-era documents. The primary repository for statewide genealogical research in Michigan. Includes lumber company records, mining records, and institutional records.

State library with extensive genealogical collections including Michigan county histories, published genealogies, city directories, newspapers on microfilm, cemetery records, and the Michigan Cemetery Source statewide index. The genealogy collection is one of the largest in the Midwest.

core_repositories.source_name: Burton Historical Collection -- Detroit Public Library
core_repositories.repository: Detroit Public Library, Main Library, Detroit
core_repositories.description: One of America's premier genealogical research libraries. Holds extensive manuscript collections, French-Canadian genealogies, Detroit city directories (1837+), newspaper indexes, family files, African American collections, ethnic community records, and the Clarence M. Burton collection of Detroit and Michigan history. Essential for Wayne County and southeastern Michigan research.

core_repositories.source_name: Bentley Historical Library -- University of Michigan
core_repositories.repository: Bentley Historical Library, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor
core_repositories.description: Major research library holding Michigan and University of Michigan historical collections. Fur trade records, personal and family papers, organizational records, political papers, and photograph collections. The American Fur Company records and other fur trade documentation are essential for early Michigan research.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable Michigan collections including vital records, probate records, county records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Michigan county offices and the Archives of Michigan. Key collections include Michigan marriages (1868-1925), deaths (1867-1952), and county-level probate and deed records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Michigan's unique record types and provides research strategies for each county.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Michigan newspapers from the territorial era to the early 20th century. Includes Detroit Free Press, Grand Rapids Press, and county weeklies. Legal notices, obituaries, marriage announcements, and community news.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Michigan / Clarke Historical Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Growing digital collection of Michigan newspapers. Clarke Historical Library at Central Michigan University manages the project. Includes papers from across the state covering local news, legal notices, and community events.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Burton Historical Collection (DPL) / Archives of Michigan / Bentley Historical Library
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Michigan was a free state from the Northwest Ordinance (1787), but slavery existed under French and British rule until the 1830s. Collections document the Underground Railroad (major routes through Cass, Berrien, Washtenaw, Wayne counties to Canada), African American church records (AME, Baptist), Freedmen's communities, and the Great Migration (1910s-1970s) that brought hundreds of thousands of Southern Black families to Detroit, Flint, Grand Rapids, and other industrial cities. The Burton Historical Collection has the most extensive African American genealogical resources in Michigan.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Michigan Native American Records -- Ottawa, Ojibwe, Potawatomi
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA (Great Lakes Agency) / Tribal Government Offices / Archives of Michigan
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Michigan's Native American communities -- Ottawa (Odawa), Ojibwe (Chippewa), and Potawatomi -- have extensive treaty, census, and allotment records. NARA holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records (Great Lakes Agency) including annuity rolls, allotment records, and school records. The Treaty of Detroit (1807), Treaty of Saginaw (1819), Treaty of Chicago (1821, 1833), and numerous other treaties affected Michigan lands. Current tribal governments (12 federally recognized tribes in Michigan) maintain enrollment records. The Archives of Michigan holds related territorial and state records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Michigan Copper Country and Upper Peninsula Tribal Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Keweenaw Bay Indian Community / Sault Ste. Marie Tribe / Bay Mills Indian Community
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Upper Peninsula tribal communities maintained continuous presence through the treaty and allotment periods. The Keweenaw Bay Indian Community (L'Anse), Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians, and Bay Mills Indian Community have tribal enrollment records and historical documentation. Catholic mission records from the Jesuit era (1660s+) document early tribal-European interactions.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Michigan Naturalization Records -- Archives of Michigan / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Archives of Michigan, Lansing / NARA Great Lakes Region, Chicago
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization petitions and declarations of intention filed in Michigan courts. Archives of Michigan holds county-level naturalization records. NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago) has federal court naturalization records for Michigan. Detroit was a major port of entry from Canada. Michigan attracted diverse immigrant communities: Finnish (Upper Peninsula), Dutch (western Michigan), Polish (Detroit, Hamtramck), German (statewide), Cornish and Italian (mining), Swedish and Norwegian (UP lumber and mining), and many others.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Detroit Port of Entry and Border Crossing Records
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Great Lakes Region, Chicago / Ancestry / FamilySearch
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Detroit was a major port of entry, especially from Canada. Border crossing records document Canadian immigrants entering through Detroit, Port Huron, and Sault Ste. Marie. Soundex indexes to border crossing entries at NARA. Ancestry and FamilySearch have digitized many border crossing records.

military_records.source_name: Michigan Military Records -- Archives of Michigan / NARA
military_records.description: Archives of Michigan holds state militia records, adjutant general records, and service records. Michigan contributed over 90,000 soldiers to the Union in the Civil War. War of 1812 records (Battle of the River Raisin, Fort Detroit). Civil War service records including the 1st Michigan Sharpshooters and US Colored Troops. Spanish-American War, WWI, and WWII draft registrations. Grand Army of the Republic post records for veterans.

land_and_property.source_name: Michigan Land Records -- Register of Deeds / BLM GLO
land_and_property.repository: County Register of Deeds offices / BLM General Land Office
land_and_property.description: Michigan is a public land state -- original land was surveyed by the federal government and sold through land offices. Federal land patents available free online at BLM GLO. Each of Michigan's 83 counties has a Register of Deeds office holding subsequent land transactions (deeds, mortgages, liens). Some counties have online access for recent records. French colonial-era land claims in the Detroit area predate the public land system and are documented at the Archives of Michigan.

vital_records.source_name: Michigan Vital Records -- Archives of Michigan / DHHS
vital_records.repository: Archives of Michigan, Lansing / Michigan Dept. of Health and Human Services
vital_records.description: Statewide vital records registration began in 1867 for births, deaths, and marriages. Compliance was poor in rural areas until after 1900. Archives of Michigan holds historical vital records (births, deaths, marriages). Modern vital records from Michigan DHHS. Pre-1867 vital events documented in church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices. County Clerks issued marriage licenses from county organization.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Archives of Michigan / Archdiocese of Detroit / FamilySearch / Calvin University Heritage Hall / Joint Archives of Holland
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-1867 Michigan research. Catholic parish records from the French colonial era (Ste. Anne de Detroit, 1704). Dutch Reformed (RCA/CRC) records from western Michigan (1847+). Finnish Lutheran records from the Upper Peninsula. German Lutheran and Catholic parish records statewide. Methodist, Baptist, Congregational, and Presbyterian records from early settlement. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Michigan church records.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Michigan. Coverage varies by county. The Library of Michigan maintains the Michigan Cemetery Source statewide index. Local historical and genealogical societies have published cemetery survey volumes.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Umbrella organization for Michigan genealogical societies. Provides research guides, workshop information, and links to county-level genealogical societies across Michigan.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Detroit Society for Genealogical Research, Burton Historical Collection
research_guides_and_societies.description: Premier genealogical society for southeastern Michigan. Publishes the Detroit Society for Genealogical Research Magazine. Maintains French-Canadian genealogical resources, ethnic community records, and research guides for Wayne County and Detroit.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Western Michigan Genealogical Society, Grand Rapids
research_guides_and_societies.description: Genealogical society serving western Michigan. Research guides, surname indexes, and publications for Kent County and surrounding areas.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Michigan's county system evolved significantly from territorial organization to statehood and beyond. Several early counties were renamed or reorganized: Brown County (1818, renamed Schoolcraft and later divided), Michilimackinac County (1818, renamed Mackinac 1843), Tonedagana (renamed Emmet), Omeena (renamed Manistee), Kaykakee (renamed Wexford), Shawano (renamed Crawford), Keskkauko (renamed Charlevoix), Aishcum (renamed Lake), and others. The county organization of the Lower and Upper Peninsulas continued through the 1890s as frontier areas became populated. Isle Royale was originally part of Keweenaw County. The Toledo War (1835-36) between Michigan and Ohio resulted in the Toledo Strip going to Ohio and Michigan receiving the Upper Peninsula as compensation at statehood in 1837.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Michigan has 5 counties with significant record losses. The 1881 Great Thumb Fire devastated three counties (Huron, Sanilac, Tuscola) in the Thumb region, destroying courthouses and records. Clare County lost records in an 1877 courthouse fire. Ontonagon County in the Upper Peninsula lost records in an 1896 fire. For burned counties, researchers should check the Archives of Michigan for microfilmed records and tax rolls that may have survived, BLM GLO for land patents (unaffected by fires), and church records which often survived courthouse fires.

burned_jurisdictions.notes: Courthouse fire destroyed many early records
Great Thumb Fire destroyed courthouse and many records
Fire destroyed courthouse and many records
Great Thumb Fire caused significant record losses
Great Thumb Fire damaged some records
